coredata.dc:title: ZnIn2S4 supported carbon dots/Ni3P hybrid for efficient photothermal catalytic hydrogen production from water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Juan
Guo, Xiaomin
Tang, Ying
Zeng, Wenjing
Zeng, Hui
Rui, Zebao
coredata.dc:description: Construction of efficient and economically controllable catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen production to relieve environmental and energy pressures remains an ongoing challenge. Herein, indium zinc sulfide supported carbon dots (CDs) and Ni3P hybrid (CDs/Ni3P/ZnIn2S4) is designed as a robust catalyst for photothermal catalytic hydrogen production by water splitting. The co-introduction of CDs/Ni3P on ZnIn2S4 as electron acceptors effectively enhances the photon harvest capacity and separation efficiency of photogenerated charges, and ultimately leads to excellent photothermal catalytic hydrogen production performance. The average hydrogen production rate of CDs/Ni3P/ZnIn2S4 reaches 1880.78 μmol g−1 h−1 under 50 °C and full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2, which is ∼8.8 times higher than the pristine ZnIn2S4 and ranges among those over noble metal catalysts. Such a work provides a reliable route for the design of non-precious photothermal catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnIn2S4
Photothermal catalysis
Hydrogen production
Carbon dots
Ni3P

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal catalytic activity of H2 generation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation schematics of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS and (b) XRD patterns of the samples.
SEM images of (a) ZIS, (b) Ni3P, (c) Ni3P/ZIS and (d) CDs/Ni3P/ZIS; (e–j) SEM-EDS images of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS; (k)TEM and (l) HRTEM images of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS.
XPS spectra of ZIS, Ni3P, CDs/Ni3P, Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS: (a) Zn 2p, (b) In 3 d, (c) S 2p, (d) Ni 2p, (e) P 2p and (f) C 1s.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of samples, (b) MS curve (illustrated as Tauc plot) of ZIS and (c) potential charge migration routes of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS.
(a) EIS spectra, (b) PL spectra, (c) TRPL spectra and (d) photocurrent response curves of ZIS, Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS under full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2.

TEMPO-ESR spectra of samples under (a) dark at room temperature (or Dark & RT), (b) dark at 50 °C (or Dark & 50 °C), (c) full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 at room temperature (or Light & RT) and (d) full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 at 50 °C (or Light & 50 °C).

(a) Hydrogen production curves over time and (b) PHE and STH efficiency after 4 h of reaction over the catalysts under full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 and 50 °C; (c) hydrogen production curves over time and (d) PHE and STH efficiency after 4 h of reaction over CDs/Ni3P/ZIS under thermal catalysis (TC, 50 °C), photocatalysis (PC, full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 at 30 °C) and photothermal catalysis (PTC, full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 and 50 °C).
XPS data of Ni 2p in Ni3P, Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS.
Performance comparison of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS and recently reported catalysts.

row.entry: 100 mL (0.35 mol L−1 Na2S and 0.25 mol L−1 Na2SO3)

head.ce:title: ZnIn2S4 supported carbon dots/Ni3P hybrid for efficient photothermal catalytic hydrogen production from water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Guangdong Engineering Technology Research Center for Platform Chemicals from Marine Biomass and Their Functionalization, Sun Yat-sen University, Zhuhai, 519082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology
Guangdong Engineering Technology Research Center for Platform Chemicals from Marine Biomass and Their Functionalization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Guangdong Engineering Technology Research Center for Platform Chemicals from Marine Biomass and Their Functionalization, Sun Yat-sen University, Zhuhai, 519082, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of efficient and economically controllable catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen production to relieve environmental and energy pressures remains an ongoing challenge. Herein, indium zinc sulfide supported carbon dots (CDs) and Ni3P hybrid (CDs/Ni3P/ZnIn2S4) is designed as a robust catalyst for photothermal catalytic hydrogen production by water splitting. The co-introduction of CDs/Ni3P on ZnIn2S4 as electron acceptors effectively enhances the photon harvest capacity and separation efficiency of photogenerated charges, and ultimately leads to excellent photothermal catalytic hydrogen production performance. The average hydrogen production rate of CDs/Ni3P/ZnIn2S4 reaches 1880.78 μmol g−1 h−1 under 50 °C and full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2, which is ∼8.8 times higher than the pristine ZnIn2S4 and ranges among those over noble metal catalysts. Such a work provides a reliable route for the design of non-precious photothermal catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:para: In consideration of the environmental and climate problems associated with the use of nonrenewable fossil fuels, solar-powered photocatalytic decomposition of water has emerged as one of the most promising green routes to produce hydrogen energy [1–3]. Various semiconductors have become the center of research in photocatalytic hydrogen production. Among them, metal sulfide semiconductor compounds exhibit perspective photocatalytic hydrogen generation performance benefiting from their suitable band structures, visible solar response, relatively mild preparation conditions, and stable photoelectrochemical properties [4,5]. As one of the important ternary sulfide semiconductors, ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) possesses a promising application in the field of photocatalytic H2 production due to its low toxicity, suitable bandgap (∼2.4 eV) and unique electronic structure, as well as relatively high chemical stability [6,7]. However, the severe photogenerated charges combination and inferior light-absorption capacity of ZIS restrict its application in photocatalytic hydrogen production [8]. Various strategies, such as morphology modulation, defect structure and addition of co-catalysts, have been employed to enhance the photocatalytic performance of ZIS, among which the loading of noble metal co-catalysts displayed superior advantages [9–11]. Wang et al. [12] anchored Pd cationic in ZIS nanostructures via cation-exchange strategy to facilitate the transfer of photogenerated carriers, and the hydrogen production rate of the optimized photocatalyst reached 1236.4 μmol g−1 h−1. Yu et al. [13] reported the combination of spherical-like ZnIn2S4 and nickel/palladium bimetallic nanoparticles to form Schottky heterojunction, which exhibits enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency of 107.7 μmol h−1. Nevertheless, the exorbitant cost of noble metals hinders the practical application. Development of affordable and efficient non-precious co-catalysts for improving the performance of potential ZIS photocatalysts is still highly desirable.

Transition metal phosphides are expected to be potential non-precious metal co-catalysts in photocatalysis because of their favorable chemical and thermal stability, unique properties with the positively charged transition metal and negatively charged phosphorus, and metal-like characteristics [14,15]. Particularly, amorphous Ni3P is typically regarded as a hopeful co-catalyst benefitting from its high electrical conductivity and special photoelectric properties [16]. Zhao et al. [17] applied Ni3P/Ni as a non-precious metal multifunctional co-catalyst on CdS by molten salt method to promote the electron trapping ability, which achieved a photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 11.8 mmol h−1 g−1 under visible light irradiation. Additionally, thermally assisted photothermal catalysis has been recognized as an innovative technology for the efficient use of solar energy, which could also be of great benefit in improving photocatalytic performance [18]. Zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent photothermal conversion capabilities for highly efficient use of solar spectrum, and the ability to combine with semiconductors expands the field of application [19,20]. Moreover, CDs have been used to improve the catalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts due to their large specific surface area, good electrical conductivity, as well as enhanced light absorption capability [21–23]. Lu et al. [24] loaded carbon dots on black g-C3N4 nanosheets by calcination method for achieving efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production with the assistance of carbon-based materials. Lu et al. [25] reported that the loading of CDs onto hollow tubular carbon nitride surfaces not only promoted carrier separation but also increased the surface temperature of the reaction system through the photothermal effect, thus improving the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity.

Taking into the promotion effect of CDs and amorphous Ni3P to light absorption, photothermal effect, and charges transfer/separation, herein, ZnIn2S4 supported CDs/Ni3P hybrid (or CDs/Ni3P/ZIS) is developed as an advanced non-precious catalyst for photothermal catalytic hydrogen production. A high H2 production rate of 1880.78 μmol g−1 h−1 under 50 °C and full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 is obtained on CDs/Ni3P/ZIS, which is among those of ZIS immobilized noble metal catalysts. The effects of CDs/Ni3P on photon harvest, charge transfer and separation, photothermal response, and photothermal catalytic hydrogen production performance over CDs/Ni3P/ZnIn2S4 are also analyzed.

Zinc chloride (ZnCl2, ≥98%), indium trichloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O, 99.9%), thioacetamide (C2H5NS, ≥99%), sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, C10H16N2O8) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Aspartic acid (C4H7NO4, >98%) was provided by Alfa Aesar. Nickel chloride (NiCl2, 99%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Nafion perfluoro polymer resin solution was purchased from DuPont. Anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O, 99.7%) and nitrogen (N2, 99.999%) were purchased from General-Reagent and Zhuhai Huaxin Gas Company, respectively. All chemicals were used without further treatment.

Carbon dots (or CDs) were prepared by referring to the literature with some modifications [26]. Briefly, 5 g aspartic acid was calcined in air at 320 °C for 100 h. The solid product was then dissolved in 100 mL deionized water containing 2.5 mL NaOH (5 mol L−1) to obtain carbon dots solution.

According to the literature [27], 0.1 mol L−1 NaH2PO2 solution and 0.1 mol L−1 NiCl2 solution were added into a beaker with a ratio of 2:1 under vigorous stirring at 600 rpm, followed by the addition of 4 g NaBH4 tardily and continuous reaction for 4 h at room temperature. After centrifugation and washing with deionized water, the amorphous Ni3P was obtained.

ZnIn2S4 was prepared by referring to our previous work [28]. 1 mmol of ZnCl2, 2 mmol of InCl3·4H2O and 8 mmol of thioacetamide were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water and stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min. The mixture was then hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 18 h. The product was washed with deionized water and ethanol thrice, respectively, and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight to obtain ZnIn2S4 (or ZIS). CDs/Ni3P composites were obtained by sonication of 0.3 g Ni3P and 1.365 mL CDs solution for 30 min, followed by drying at 60 °C and calcination at 500 °C for 1 h under the nitrogen atmosphere. Then, equal mass quality of ZnIn2S4 and CDs/Ni3P or Ni3P were ground together in a mortar with the assistance of ethanol for 30 min to obtain CDs/Ni3P/ZIS or Ni3P/ZIS.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV) from Rigaku Japan was used to analyze the crystal structure of the samples. The morphology of the samples was observed by a multifunction scanning electron microscope (SEM, ThermoFisher Apreo 2 S) and a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30) from FEI. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained using a UV–vis–NIR spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere and BaSO4 as the reference sample. Thermo Fisher Scientific X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Nexsa) was used to characterize the surface chemical state of the samples. Photoluminescence spectroscopy measurements were operated on a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000). Photogenerated electron spin resonance signals were measured using an electron spin resonance spectrometer (ESR, JES X320). Photoelectrochemical tests were conducted using a standard three-electrode system on a Chenhua electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) with Pt electrode as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, respectively.

For a typical photothermocatalytic test, 5 mg catalyst was added into the quartz reactor containing 5 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 EDTA solution and sonicated for 5 min. Ar was passed into the reactor to remove air before the reaction. The photothermal catalytic reaction was operated at 50 °C with light intensity of 200 mW cm−2 using a 300 W Xenon lamp as the light source. The H2 production during the reaction was detected by a gas chromatograph (GC, GC1690) equipped with TCD detector and Ar as carrier gas. The solar-H2 conversion efficiency (STH, %) of photothermal catalytic hydrogen production was calculated with the following equation [29].
S T H =
R
(
H 2
)
× Δ G
P × S
× 100 %
herein, R (H2) (mol s−1) represents the reaction rate of hydrogen production, G (J mol−1) refers to the Gibbs free energy of the reaction in which water decomposes to produce H2 and O2, S (cm2) and P (W cm−2) are the irradiation area and intensity, respectively.

Herein, CDs/Ni3P/ZIS was fabricated by a multistage assembly method. As schematically shown in Fig. 1 a, the precursors CDs and amorphous Ni3P were firstly synthesized by calcination and chemical reduction, respectively. Meanwhile, ZIS was prepared via hydrothermal measurement. Then, CDs/Ni3P/ZIS was obtained by the assemble of CDs/Ni3P and ZIS through mechanical grinding.

XRD patterns of the samples are listed in Fig. 1b. A broad peak at 40°–50° is observed in the pattern of Ni3P sample, indicating the successful fabrication of amorphous Ni3P structure [30]. After the combination of CDs to Ni3P, a significant diffraction peak at 27.7° attributed to CDs, the diffraction peaks from metallic nickel at 44.3°, 51.9° and 76.4°, and the weak diffraction peaks assigned to Ni3P at 36.4°, 41.9° and 46.5° are observed in CDs/Ni3P [17,31]. The findings indicate that Ni3P particles are partly reduced in-situ under N2 atmosphere thereby resulting in the formation of complex particles. The diffraction peaks of CDs/Ni3P near 45° and 52° in CDs/Ni3P/ZIS illustrate the successful incorporation of CDs/Ni3P onto ZIS. Moreover, the diffraction peaks at 21.6° (006), 27.7° (102), 30.5° (104), 47.3° (112), 52.4° (116) and 55.7° (022) match well with the reported hexagonal ZnIn2S4, demonstrating the successful synthesis of ZIS [32]. After the incorporation of Ni3P or CDs/Ni3P, the diffraction peaks of ZIS are retained in Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS, which suggests the negligible influence on the stable crystal structure of ZIS.

SEM image in Fig. 2 a shows that ZIS exhibits flower-like microsphere structure composed of nanosheets with a uniform diameter of 2∼3 μm. Ni3P appears as irregular nanoparticles with a diameter of 30 nm and exhibits slight agglomeration (Fig. 2b). After the incorporation of Ni3P or CDs/Ni3P, the rough flower-like microsphere surface is observed in Ni3P/ZIS or CDs/Ni3P/ZIS (Fig. 2c & d), which are considerably different from the sharp micro-flower structure in ZIS. Uniformly dispersed Zn, In, S, P, Ni and C elements in CDs/Ni3P/ZIS are demonstrated by EDS images in Fig. 2e–j. TEM image in Fig. 2k shows that CDs/Ni3P/ZIS has clear nanosheets outline. HRTEM image in Fig. 2l clearly shows the loading of the nanoparticles with a diameter of 30 nm on the surface of ZIS microspheres. The lattice fringes of 0.41 and 0.34 nm corresponding to the (003) and (011) crystal planes of hexagonal ZIS and the 0.24 nm of lattice stripes referring to the (031) crystal plane of Ni3P collectively expose the existence of heterogeneous interface among the components in CDs/Ni3P/ZIS [17,28].

UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 4 a demonstrate an exceptional photon harvest capability across the UV–vis–NIR region of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS. By comparison, the ZIS shows an absorbance threshold near 600 nm. In line with the UV–vis–NIR absorption enhancement, the loading of CDs/Ni3P also changes the sample color from bright orange for ZIS and grey-black for Ni3P/ZIS to grey-green for CDs/Ni3P/ZIS. To further deduce the electronic structure of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS, the Kubelka-Munk formula was subsequently applied to calculate the bandgap (E g) of ZIS, and this value was deduced to be 2.47 eV (Fig. 4b) [39]. The positive slope of Mott-Schottky (MS) curve verifies the n-type semiconductor property of ZIS (Fig. 4b) [40]. The flat band potential (E FB) of −1.00 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) can be converted to −0.80 V (vs. NHE) according to the formula E NHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.197. Ordinarily, the deviation between conduction band potential (E CB) and E FB of n-type semiconductors is 0.2 eV, whereas a specific relation equation E VB = E CB + E g is satisfied between conduction band potential (E CB) and valence band potential (E VB) [41]. Therefore, the E CB and E VB of ZIS are calculated as −1.00 eV and 1.47 eV, respectively. According to the literature, the work functions of ZIS and Ni3P are about 4.41 and 4.75 eV, which means electrons near the heterogeneous interface may migrate from ZIS to Ni3P or CDs/Ni3P [42,43]. Based on the analysis, the band structure of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS is plotted in Fig. 4c to raise potential charge transfer mechanism. Under light irradiation, photogenerated carriers are photoexcited and spatially separated in ZIS. Subsequently, these free carriers migrate to the conduction and valence bands, respectively. The work function discrepancy between ZIS and CDs/Ni3P creates a potential difference that contributes to the reconstruction of electrical field and further builds Schottky barrier. The broadened solar spectrum absorption range and enhanced light absorbance of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS promote the photoinduced charges production, and the CDs/Ni3P hybrid serves as an electron acceptor to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Additionally, localized surface plasmon resonance of Ni3P and photothermal conversion of CDs co-raise the temperature of catalyst to stimulate the production of energetic carriers with high redox capacity, which are expected to favor the photocatalytic decomposition of water for hydrogen production reaction [28].

Electrochemical impedance (EIS) spectra of the samples are compared in Fig. 5 a. The Nyquist plots show a Nyquist semicircular arc radius sequence CDs/Ni3P/ZIS < Ni3P/ZIS < ZIS, indicating a lower charge transfer resistance of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS, which benefits the photogenerated electron-hole (or e−-h+) transfer and separation [44]. Photoexcited surface charge separation properties were analyzed by steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (PL) detection (Fig. 5b). At the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, there is a major broad peak near 540 nm in the PL emission peaks of ZIS, Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS. The height of the fluorescence emission peak holds an order of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS < Ni3P/ZIS < ZIS, indicating the lower photoinduced e−-h+ recombination rate in CDs/Ni3P/ZIS [45]. Apparently, the tight heterogeneous interface between CDs/Ni3P and ZIS improves photogenerated electron transport on ZIS and promotes efficient e−-h+ separation on the catalyst. Transient fluorescence spectroscopy (TRPL) was employed to invest the fluorescence lifetime of photogenerated charges (Fig. 5c). The estimated fluorescence lifetime decay value of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS (∼3.19 ns) is remarkably larger than that of ZIS (∼2.95 ns) and Ni3P/ZIS (∼2.35 ns). The larger fluorescence lifetime of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS indicates a slower decay of photogenerated electrons in the samples, which is more conducive to electron migration and photocatalytic reaction [46]. Photocurrent response density measurements show that all samples exhibit photocurrent response under full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 during five irradiation cycles (Fig. 5d). In the repetitive process of turning on-off the light source, ZIS presents a significant decrease in the photocurrent density due to the severe charge complexation caused by the structural defects of ZIS [39]. As expected, CDs/Ni3P/ZIS shows significantly higher photocurrent density than ZIS and Ni3P/ZIS in consideration of the stronger UV–vis–NIR absorption and better photogenerated carriers separation efficiency of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS [47]. CDs/Ni3P/ZIS also keeps a stable photocurrent density after multiple switching irradiations, demonstrating its excellent stability while maintaining efficient carrier separation.

Subsequently, ESR measurements were performed to further explore the charge transfer and separation ability of the samples under different conditions (Fig. 6 ). The ESR signal intensity indicates the electron transfer behavior from sample to spin trapping agent 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO), and the sharper decaying signals means faster migration rate [48]. In dark and room temperature (or Dark & RT) conditions, a triplet-state ESR signal originating from TEMPO is observed in ZIS, Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS, respectively (Fig. 6a). Under darkness at 50 °C (or Dark & 50 °C), extremely slight decay of ESR signals in each sample illustrates that low temperature heat contributes marginally to charge generation and transfer (Fig. 6b). Comparatively, obvious ESR signal intensity attenuation can be found in these samples under illumination with 200 mW cm−2 at room temperature (or Light & RT) for 12 min (Fig. 6c), verifying that numerous charge carriers are photoexcited and separated to react with TEMPO. Meanwhile, the weakest ESR signal of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS confirms the intimate heterojunction interface endows CDs/Ni3P/ZIS with the most exceptional photon harvest capability and photoelectronic response performance. Under 200 mW cm−2 irradiation at 50 °C (or Light & 50 °C), signal intensity in all samples reduces substantially and the signal in CDs/Ni3P/ZIS virtually disappears. This implies that the exogenous heat can thermally activate electron-hole pairs into highly-active energetic carriers for efficient spatial separation and activity. Such a photothermal synergistic effect plays a crucial role in intensifying photoelectron extraction dynamics and accelerating the surface reaction behaviors (Fig. 6d) [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal catalytic activity of H2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal catalytic H2 production performance of ZIS, Ni3P/ZIS and CDs/Ni3P/ZIS under full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 at 50 °C is compared in Fig. 7 a & b. The average photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency (PHE) of pristine ZIS maintains a low level of 213.38 μmol g−1 h−1 and markedly increases to 1358.90 μmol g−1 h−1 after loading Ni3P on the surface of ZIS, which exceeds the sum of Ni3P and ZIS. Hydrogen production and PHE of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS are monitored to be 7117.49 μmol g−1 and 1880.78 μmol g−1 h−1 within 4 h, respectively, which is 9.3 and 8.8 times higher than that of ZIS (768.83 μmol g−1 and 213.38 μmol g−1 h−1) and is close to those noble metal promoted ZIS catalysts [12]. PHE and STH values gradually increase from ZIS→Ni3P→Ni3P/ZIS→CDs/Ni3P/ZIS, and the STH value of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS reaches 0.06% (Fig. 7b).

The hydrogen production performance of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS under different reaction conditions is further compared (Fig. 7c & d). Thermocatalysis test (TC, 50 °C) records a low hydrogen generation of 562.76 μmol g−1 after 4 h of reaction, revealing that heat treatment cannot trigger the efficient hydrogen production activity of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS. Under the condition of photocatalysis (PC, full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 at 30 °C), the H2 yield of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS achieves 5086.67 μmol g−1, indicating that the photocatalytic H2 evolution property of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS can be stimulated under light irradiation. The hydrogen production efficiency of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS by photothermal catalysis (PTC, full spectrum irradiation of 200 mW cm−2 and 50 °C) reaches 1880.78 μmol g−1 h−1, which is much higher than that of photocatalysis (1334.85 μmol g−1 h−1), thermocatalysis (240.80 μmol g−1 h−1) and the sum of them, indicating that the transport and separation process of photoexcited carriers and interface reaction rate can be further improved by photothermal synergy. The photothermal catalytic H2 generation rate of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS is compared with the reported state-of-the-art catalysts, which shows that non-precious metal ZnIn2S4-based catalyst CDs/Ni3P/ZIS exhibits satisfactory hydrogen production capacity, which is even better than those noble metal catalysts [12,50,51,54] (Table 2 ).

Comprehensive characterizations illustrate that intimate interfacial bridging between CDs/Ni3P and ZIS benefits effective electron transfer. The photoelectrochemical analysis and experiment results indicate that CDs/Ni3P/ZIS achieves superior light absorption capacity, excellent charge transfer/separation ability, exceptional photoelectron extraction efficiency and accelerating surface reaction dynamics. These astonishing advantages endow CDs/Ni3P/ZIS with superior photothermal catalytic activity to exhibit satisfactory hydrogen production performance in the absence of precious metals.

In summary, a novel noble metal free catalyst CDs/Ni3P/ZnIn2S4 (or CDs/Ni3P/ZIS) having excellent photon harvest capacity and favorable electron extraction efficiency is fabricated by multistage assembly mothed for efficient H2 production via photothermal catalytic water splitting. In this catalyst, intimate interfacial bridging of CDs/Ni3P hybrid and ZIS facilitates the transport/separation of photogenerated carriers, enhancement of light-absorption capacity and photoelectric catalytic activity, which can be further promoted by photothermal conditions. Benefitting from these astonishing aspects, the photothermal catalytic H2 production rate of CDs/Ni3P/ZIS is recorded as 1880.78 μmol g−1 h−1 without the employment of precious metals. This work offers a potential route to construct efficient non-precious photothermal catalysts for green hydrogen production.

Juan Jia: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaomin Guo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ying Tang: Writing – review & editing. Wenjing Zeng: Writing – review & editing. Hui Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Zebao Rui: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support by the Natural Science Funds of Guangdong for Distinguished Young Scholar (No. 2022B1515020098) and Science &Technology Program of Guangzhou(No. 2023B03J0004) is appreciated.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-thermo catalytic hydrogen production along with pollutant degradation by cost-effective carbon materials
J Energy Storage
